name: Mars Viking Labeled Release
description: The Viking Labeled Release (LR) experiment, part of NASA's 1976 Viking mission to Mars, is at the heart of a long-standing debate about whether life was detected on Mars. The LR experiment involved adding nutrient solution to Martian soil samples and monitoring for gas release, which would indicate metabolism. Proponents of the 'life on Mars' interpretation argue that the experiment yielded positive results – a release of carbon dioxide – indicating biological activity. The experiment was conducted at two landing sites, and both produced similar positive results. The lead scientist of the LR experiment, Gilbert Levin, has consistently maintained that the results constitute evidence of life. This interpretation is often coupled with arguments that NASA has suppressed or downplayed this evidence. Mainstream scientific consensus, however, attributes the LR results to non-biological chemical reactions in the Martian soil, specifically the presence of highly reactive oxidants like perchlorates. These oxidants, when mixed with the nutrient solution, could have produced the observed gas release without the presence of life. This interpretation is supported by subsequent Mars missions, such as the Phoenix lander, which confirmed the presence of perchlorates in Martian soil. Critics of the 'life on Mars' claim also point to the absence of organic molecules detected by other Viking instruments, which would be expected if life were present. The debate continues, fueled by ongoing discoveries about Mars and the limitations of the Viking-era technology. Newer theories suggest that the oxidants might have destroyed the organic molecules, or that the original assumptions about Martian life were too Earth-centric.
subtitle: Debate over Viking mission's life detection

claims.claim: The Labeled Release experiment detected metabolism in Martian soil.
claims.evidence: Release of carbon dioxide after nutrient addition.
claims.criticism: Non-biological chemical reactions, particularly with perchlorates, can explain the gas release.

claims.claim: NASA has suppressed or downplayed evidence of life on Mars.
claims.evidence: Gilbert Levin's continued advocacy and perceived lack of follow-up research.
claims.criticism: NASA followed scientific consensus and continued Mars exploration with different instruments and objectives.

claims.claim: The absence of detected organic molecules is due to their destruction by Martian oxidants.
claims.evidence: Discovery of perchlorates on Mars and their known oxidizing properties.
claims.criticism: Alternative explanations for the lack of organics, such as their rarity or different forms than expected.

keyFigures.name: Gilbert Levin
keyFigures.role: Lead scientist of the Viking Labeled Release experiment

keyFigures.name: Norman Horowitz
keyFigures.role: Viking mission scientist who argued against the life interpretation

keyFigures.name: Chris McKay
keyFigures.role: Astrobiologist who studies the possibility of life on Mars and has commented on the LR results

mythologyConnections: Ares/Mars as a god of war and potential origin of life

timeline.event: Viking 1 and 2 land on Mars and conduct the Labeled Release experiment.
Debate continues over the interpretation of the Labeled Release results.
Phoenix lander confirms the presence of perchlorates in Martian soil.
Continued Mars exploration and research into the possibility of past or present life.

sources.author: Gilbert V. Levin

sources.title: Gilbert V. Levin
